title: Go Arrays
content:
### Arrays in Go

Arrays are fixed-length sequences of elements of the same type.

### Declaration

```go
var nums [5]int             // array of 5 integers, all zero
var nums2 [3]int = [3]int{1, 2, 3}
nums3 := [4]int{10, 20, 30, 40}
nums4 := [...]int{1, 2, 3}  // compiler counts elements
```

### Accessing Elements

```go
arr := [3]string{"a", "b", "c"}
fmt.Println(arr[0]) // "a"
arr[1] = "x"
fmt.Println(arr)    // ["a", "x", "c"]
```

### Length

```go
arr := [5]int{1, 2, 3}
fmt.Println(len(arr)) // 5
```

### Array of Arrays

```go
matrix := [2][3]int{
    {1, 2, 3},
    {4, 5, 6},
}
```

### Arrays are Values

In Go, arrays are values, not pointers. Assigning or passing an array copies all elements:

```go
a := [3]int{1, 2, 3}
b := a  // copies the entire array
b[0] = 99
fmt.Println(a[0]) // 1 (unchanged)
```

### Array Literals

```go
// With index initialization
arr := [5]int{0: 10, 4: 50}
// Result: [10, 0, 0, 0, 50]
```

### Comparing Arrays

Arrays are comparable if the element type is comparable:

```go
a := [3]int{1, 2, 3}
b := [3]int{1, 2, 3}
fmt.Println(a == b) // true
```

| Feature | Detail |
|---------|--------|
| Fixed size | Yes, part of the type |
| `[3]int` and `[5]int` | Different types! |
| Copy semantics | Yes, pass by value |
| Comparable | Yes (if elements are) |

> **Key Point:** In practice, slices are used far more often than arrays in Go. Arrays are mainly used as building blocks for slices or when fixed-size buffers are needed.

quiz.question: Are `[3]int` and `[5]int` the same type?
options: No, size is part of the type
Yes, if used in the same package

quiz.question: What happens when you assign an array to another variable?
